summary: Cha is a pragmatic final-year computing student who has developed highly functional eating habits around efficiency and convenience. She approaches food with an almost engineering mindset - everything must be portable, quick to prepare, and minimal cleanup. Her preference for chicken wraps, pasta, and meal prep reflects someone who sees eating as fuel rather than pleasure during busy periods. She's socially reserved, preferring small gatherings or one-on-one time over large social events, and actively avoids spending time in shared kitchen spaces with flatmates. Despite claiming to rarely order delivery, her diary reveals a more complex relationship with food sourcing - she frequently eats out and orders delivery when with her boyfriend or brother, suggesting her 'low usage' narrative may reflect social desirability bias or genuine misperception of her own habits. Her most animated moments come when discussing systemic frustrations like minimum order thresholds and grocery delivery quality control, revealing someone who thinks critically about service design and student-specific needs.
personality_notes: Reserved, analytical, and efficiency-focused. Gives thoughtful, detailed responses but avoids elaborating on social aspects. Shows strong opinions about practical issues like pricing and service quality. Appears uncomfortable with social cooking situations.
food_relationship: Food is primarily functional fuel that must be convenient, portable, and cost-effective, though she values control over ingredients and cooking when time permits.

key_moments.description: Repeatedly emphasizes wanting food that can be eaten quickly without sitting down, stored in bags, minimal cleanup
key_moments.significance: Reveals deep prioritization of efficiency over food enjoyment - suggests high academic stress or time pressure

key_moments.description: Detailed explanation of grocery delivery problems with expiry dates and quality control
key_moments.significance: Shows analytical thinking and high standards for service quality - not just price-sensitive but quality-conscious

key_moments.description: Passionate response about minimum order thresholds being unfair to students
key_moments.significance: Most emotionally engaged moment - reveals genuine frustration with systems that don't accommodate individual student needs

what_not_said: Any mention of food enjoyment or pleasure
Details about social eating beyond basic logistics
Emotional connection to food or cooking
Financial stress despite detailed budget awareness
initial_hypotheses: High academic workload drives extreme efficiency focus in food choices
Social anxiety around shared kitchen spaces influences delivery/eating out behavior
Strong analytical mindset applied to all aspects of food sourcing and budgeting
May underestimate actual delivery usage due to social desirability bias
questions_for_coding: How does academic stress specifically influence food decision-making?
What role does social anxiety play in food sourcing choices?
How do efficiency needs conflict with cost consciousness?
What triggers the shift from 'functional eating' to 'social eating' modes?
diary_summary: Actual eating pattern shows frequent meal skipping (11/21 meals), heavy reliance on eating out (most common source), and 2 delivery orders in 7 days. Breakfast consistently skipped, irregular meal timing, and social eating concentrated around boyfriend/family visits.
say_do_gaps: Claims to order delivery 'maybe twice a month' but ordered twice in one week during diary study
Emphasizes cooking and meal prep in interview but diary shows more eating out than home cooking
Describes detailed meal prep routines but diary shows frequent meal skipping and irregular eating patterns
Claims to avoid social eating but diary shows most meals were eaten with others (boyfriend, family, friends)
